wods.title: Front Squat Foundation + Glycolytic Leg Burner

movements.name: Hip Circles
movements.notes: Mobilize hip joint in all planes

movements.name: World's Greatest Stretch
movements.notes: Hip flexor and thoracic rotation opener

movements.name: Good Morning (empty barbell)
movements.notes: Hip hinge activation with posterior chain engagement

movements.name: Front Squat
movements.notes: Third working set - technique focus under load

movements.name: Front Squat
movements.notes: Top set - neural preparation for heavy loading

movements.name: Box Jump
movements.notes: Step down to preserve Achilles - power output focus

movements.name: Kettlebell Swing (Russian)
movements.notes: Hip extension power - swing to eye level

movements.name: Burpee
movements.notes: Full chest-to-deck, jump and clap at top

movements.name: Hip Flexor Stretch (90/90)
movements.notes: Address hip flexor tightness from squatting

movements.name: Foam Roll — Quads
movements.notes: Reduce quad tension and perceived soreness

movements.name: Air Squat (slow tempo)
movements.notes: Active recovery - move blood through legs

session_why.text: This session targets lower body strength with anterior chain emphasis (front squat), followed by a glycolytic-dominant metcon in the 10-15 minute time domain. The front squat builds quadriceps and core strength while demanding upright torso position and thoracic mobility. The metcon uses triple extension movements (box jump, KB swing, burpee) to maintain the lower body focus while training the glycolytic energy system. This session is appropriate for Week 1 baseline establishment in a mesocycle.
session_why.source: Haff & Triplett - NSCA Essentials 4th ed, Ch. 17
session_why.url_or_ref: Chapter on resistance training program design and exercise selection

movement_why.text: Front squat was selected as the primary lower body strength movement because it emphasizes quadriceps development, requires greater core stabilization than back squat, and builds the receiving position for Olympic lifts. The ramp progression prepares the nervous system progressively for heavier loads while reinforcing technique. The metcon movements (box jump, KB swing, burpee) were chosen to maintain lower body emphasis through hip extension patterns while avoiding direct squatting under fatigue, which would compromise technique quality.
movement_why.source: Zatsiorsky & Kraemer - Science and Practice of Strength Training, Ch. 3
movement_why.url_or_ref: Chapter on exercise classification and neural preparation

loading_why.text: The ramp/build-up progression scheme (5@70%, 4@75%, 3@78%, 2@82%) is appropriate for Week 1 baseline establishment. This intra-session progression gradually increases neural demand while decreasing reps, preparing the CNS for heavier loads and allowing technique refinement under progressively challenging weights. Rest periods are auto-regulated between 90 seconds (lower intensity) and 3 minutes (top set) to ensure adequate phosphocreatine recovery for the ATP-PC system. RPE 7-8 targets are prescribed when 1RM is unknown, ensuring submaximal loading that allows for technique focus.
loading_why.source: Zatsiorsky & Kraemer - Science and Practice of Strength Training
loading_why.url_or_ref: Progressive warm-up sets as essential for neural preparation

movements.name: Deadlift (empty barbell)
movements.notes: Movement-specific prep with neutral spine focus

movements.name: Hollow Body Hold
movements.notes: Core tension prep for heavy loading

movements.name: Deadlift
movements.notes: Wave 2 - First tier (exploits PAP from Wave 1)

movements.name: Deadlift
movements.notes: Wave 2 - Peak tier (highest load of session)

movements.name: Assault Bike
movements.notes: Push legs but preserve capacity for wall balls

movements.name: Goblet Squat
movements.notes: Squat pattern under moderate load post-deadlift

movements.name: Toes-to-Bar
movements.notes: Core endurance - scale to knees-to-chest if needed

movements.name: Foam Roll — Lats
movements.notes: Address lat tightness from deadlift grip

session_why.text: This session targets maximal strength development in the posterior chain through wave-loaded deadlifts, followed by a medium-duration metcon that maintains lower body emphasis without compromising recovery. The deadlift is the foundational hip hinge movement, recruiting the entire posterior chain (hamstrings, glutes, erectors, lats). The metcon is structured as a For Time format to drive glycolytic system development while managing volume - 5 rounds keeps total work manageable after heavy deadlifts. Appropriate for Week 2 of a mesocycle with increased intensity over Week 1.
session_why.source: Haff & Triplett - NSCA Essentials 4th ed, Ch. 17
session_why.url_or_ref: Resistance training exercise selection and posterior chain emphasis

movement_why.text: Deadlift is the primary hip hinge strength movement and recruits more total muscle mass than any other single exercise. Wave loading is used instead of straight sets to exploit post-activation potentiation - heavy sets prime the nervous system, making subsequent moderate loads feel lighter and allowing higher total volume at elevated intensities. The metcon movements (assault bike, goblet squat, toes-to-bar) maintain lower body loading through the legs and core without directly loading the posterior chain under fatigue, which would risk technique breakdown after heavy deadlifts.
movement_why.url_or_ref: PAP effects lasting 3-10 minutes after heavy loading; wave loading exploits neural priming

loading_why.text: Wave loading (75-80-83%, then 77-81-85%) is appropriate for Week 2 strength development. This scheme oscillates between heavier and lighter loads to exploit post-activation potentiation - the heavy sets increase motor unit recruitment, temporarily enhancing force production for the next wave. Rest periods of 2-3 minutes ensure adequate phosphocreatine recovery between sets. The Romanian deadlift accessory work at 40% provides hypertrophy stimulus and technical practice of the hip hinge without further CNS fatigue. This progression scheme is more advanced than straight sets and requires intermediate+ athlete readiness.
loading_why.url_or_ref: Wave loading structure and PAP exploitation for strength emphasis

wods.title: Back Squat EMOM + Short Glycolytic Metcon

movements.name: Hip Circles
movements.notes: Hip joint mobility in all planes

movements.name: Back Squat
movements.notes: Second build-up set - prepare for working weight

movements.name: Back Squat
movements.notes: Complete 3 reps at top of each 2-minute window - rest remaining time. Auto-regulated pacing with consistent load. Week 2 intensity.

movements.name: Bulgarian Split Squat (back foot elevated)
movements.notes: Accessory single-leg work for balance and unilateral strength

movements.name: Kettlebell Swing (American)
movements.notes: Full overhead swing - hip extension power

movements.name: Row (Ergometer)
movements.notes: Leg drive emphasis - drive through heels

movements.name: Burpee Box Jump Over
movements.notes: Step down on far side - preserve Achilles and maintain pace

movements.name: Hip Flexor Stretch (90/90)
movements.notes: Address hip flexor tightness from squatting

session_why.text: This session develops lower body maximal strength through back squats programmed in an EMOM format, followed by a short-duration glycolytic metcon. The back squat is the foundational lower body strength movement, recruiting quadriceps, glutes, hamstrings, and core musculature. The EMOM format creates structured pacing and auto-regulated rest while maintaining session flow. The metcon is kept to <10 minutes to target the glycolytic energy system without excessive volume post-squatting. This structure respects the concurrent training principle - strength work occurs first with full CNS availability.
session_why.source: Glassman 2002 - CrossFit Journal, What is Fitness
session_why.url_or_ref: Strength as foundational physical skill; concurrent training model

movement_why.text: Back squat was selected because it allows the highest absolute loading of any squat variation, making it ideal for maximal strength development. The E2MOM format provides consistent 2-minute intervals - athletes complete 3 reps then rest the remainder, earning more recovery if execution is faster. This builds pacing discipline and prevents sandbagging. The metcon movements (American KB swing, row, burpee box jump) maintain lower body emphasis through hip-dominant patterns while avoiding direct squatting under fatigue. The 21-15-9 rep scheme creates a glycolytic stimulus in the 6-10 minute time domain.
movement_why.url_or_ref: EMOM as alternative to timed rest; maintains session flow and auto-regulates recovery

loading_why.text: The E2MOM format at 77% 1RM (RPE 8) for 8 rounds provides 24 total working reps at Week 2 intensity. The 2-minute interval ensures adequate phosphocreatine recovery - at 77% intensity, 3 reps take approximately 15-20 seconds, leaving 100-105 seconds rest. This interval is sufficient for the ATP-PC system to recover for heavy loading (>70% 1RM requires minimum E2MOM per hard rules). The EMOM format is used here to add variety from traditional rest periods and keep the strength block engaging. Straight sets would be equally valid, but EMOM adds timer integration and mental engagement.
loading_why.url_or_ref: EMOM strength format guidelines and rest adequacy for moderate-heavy loads

wods.title: Power Clean Complex + EMOM Leg Conditioning

movements.name: Shoulder CARs
movements.notes: Shoulder joint preparation for catching position

movements.name: Hang Power Clean (empty barbell)
movements.notes: Movement-specific prep - focus on speed under bar

movements.name: Front Squat (from rack)
movements.notes: Prep front rack and legs for complex

movements.name: Power Clean + Front Squat
movements.notes: Complete 1 power clean, then perform 2 front squats without dropping the bar. 3-minute interval ensures full CNS recovery for power work. Week 1 baseline intensity.

movements.name: Goblet Squat
movements.notes: Accessory squat volume - upright torso position

movements.name: Ski Erg
movements.notes: Minute 1 - hip hinge and lat engagement

movements.name: Box Jump
movements.notes: Minute 3 - triple extension power, step down

metcon.target_score: Complete all reps within each minute with 10-15 seconds rest minimum

movements.name: Foam Roll — Lats
movements.notes: Release lat tension from catching position

session_why.text: This session targets explosive lower body power through Olympic lifting complexes, followed by a structured EMOM metcon that maintains power output across multiple energy system pathways. The power clean + front squat complex develops the full pulling movement plus anterior chain strength under load immediately after the catch. Olympic lifting sessions must be capped at 3x per week and never placed after heavy lower body work - this session follows that sequencing rule. The EMOM metcon uses 3-movement rotation to manage fatigue while maintaining lower body emphasis through hip-dominant patterns.
session_why.source: Haff & Triplett - NSCA Essentials 4th ed, Ch. 17
session_why.url_or_ref: Olympic lifting for power development; complex training for concurrent strength-power adaptation

movement_why.text: The power clean is the foundational Olympic pulling movement, developing explosive hip extension, speed under the bar, and coordination across multiple joints. Pairing it with front squats in a complex exploits post-activation potentiation - the explosive clean primes the nervous system for the squats that follow, while the squats add volume to reinforce the catch position. The E3MOM format is appropriate because Olympic lifting requires full CNS recovery between efforts - 3-minute intervals allow phosphocreatine replenishment and mental preparation. The metcon movements (ski erg, KB swing, box jump) are all hip-extension dominant, maintaining the lower body power theme without technical complexity under fatigue.
movement_why.url_or_ref: PAP effects in complex training; heavy loading enhances subsequent performance

loading_why.text: The E3MOM format at 72% of power clean 1RM provides sufficient intensity for power development (30-70% range per loading parameters) while allowing technical precision. The 3-minute interval is mandatory for Olympic lifting - power movements require full neural recovery and cannot be rushed. This is Week 1 baseline loading, establishing technique quality before intensity increases in subsequent weeks. The complex (1 clean + 2 squats) keeps total reps low to preserve movement quality. The metcon EMOM format uses 1-minute intervals with staggered movements - each station gets 2 minutes rest before repeating, which is adequate for the power-endurance stimulus without full recovery.
loading_why.url_or_ref: E3MOM for Olympic lifting; rest adequacy for power development and technical quality

wods.title: Overhead Squat Progression + Aerobic Lower Body Work

movements.name: Barbell Pass-Through (empty bar)
movements.notes: Shoulder flexion and external rotation prep

movements.name: Overhead Squat (empty barbell)
movements.notes: Movement-specific prep - focus on overhead stability

movements.name: Overhead Squat
movements.notes: Second build-up set - increase control demand

movements.name: Overhead Squat
movements.notes: Week 1 baseline - technical focus. OHS demands shoulder mobility, midline stability, and balance. Keep load moderate to preserve form.

movements.name: Strict Press
movements.notes: Accessory overhead strength - builds shoulder stability for OHS

movements.name: Hollow Body Hold
movements.notes: Core tension practice - prerequisite for overhead stability

movements.name: Row (Ergometer)
movements.notes: Aerobic base - leg drive emphasis, sustainable pace

movements.name: Air Squat
movements.notes: High-rep squat volume under fatigue - movement quality over speed

movements.name: Thoracic Rotation
movements.notes: Address thoracic stiffness from overhead work

movements.name: Foam Roll — Lats
movements.notes: Release lat tension from overhead position

session_why.text: This session develops overhead squat proficiency and shoulder stability while building aerobic capacity through monostructural cardio. The overhead squat is the most technically demanding squat variation, requiring full-body coordination, thoracic extension, shoulder mobility, and core stability. The metcon is intentionally aerobic (14-minute AMRAP at moderate pace) to provide active recovery stimulus after the technical overhead work and to fulfill the minimum 1 aerobic session per week requirement. This session fits into a weekly structure where heavy posterior chain work occurred 48+ hours prior, respecting CNS recovery timelines.
session_why.source: Glassman 2002 - CrossFit Journal, What is Fitness
session_why.url_or_ref: CrossFit's 10 physical skills - OHS develops multiple skills concurrently (strength, flexibility, coordination, balance)

movement_why.text: The overhead squat was selected because it is the most comprehensive lower body + midline + shoulder stability movement in CrossFit, serving as both a strength exercise and a diagnostic tool for mobility restrictions. Straight sets at moderate intensity (70% 1RM, 5x3) allow technical focus without excessive neural fatigue. The accessory strict press builds overhead strength that directly transfers to OHS stability. The metcon movements (row, bike, air squat) are all lower-body dominant but kept at aerobic pace - this maintains the category focus while training the oxidative energy system, which is distinct from the glycolytic metcons in other sessions this week.
movement_why.source: Haff & Triplett - NSCA Essentials 4th ed, Ch. 3
movement_why.url_or_ref: Energy systems - oxidative system dominant beyond 3-4 minutes; aerobic base development requires sustained low-moderate intensity

loading_why.text: Straight sets at 70% 1RM (RPE 7) with 2-minute rest intervals are appropriate for Week 1 baseline establishment on a complex movement like the overhead squat. Unlike wave loading or ramp progressions, straight sets provide consistent exposure to the same load across all 5 sets, which is ideal when technical mastery is the priority. The 5x3 scheme balances volume (15 total reps) with quality - sets of 3 prevent excessive fatigue that would degrade form. This is the simplest progression scheme, making it appropriate for the most complex movement. The metcon is not programmed for intensity but for aerobic volume, fulfilling the recovery and energy system diversity principles.
loading_why.url_or_ref: Straight sets for baseline strength and hypertrophy phases; moderate intensity with moderate volume
